title: Use Omit<Interface, 'type'> for payload casts in bridge.ts subscribeToClaudeEvents
analysis:
In gui/src/bridge.ts lines 159-186, the subscribeToClaudeEvents function uses six inline anonymous type casts (e.g., `{ id: string; content: string }`) that duplicate field definitions already declared in the exported interfaces in gui/sidecar/src/types.ts (TextDelta, ThinkingDelta, ToolCall, ToolResult, Done, ErrorMessage). If a field is added or renamed in a constituent interface, these inline casts silently fall out of sync.

The fix is purely type-level: expand the import on line 20 to bring in the six interfaces, then replace each inline cast with `Omit<Interface, 'type'>`. The `p` variable is also renamed to `payload` per codebase convention.

Existing tests in claudeBridge.test.ts are comprehensive — they already verify all 7 event type mappings, rollback behavior, and edge cases (extra fields, rogue type fields). Since the behavioral contract is unchanged, all existing tests must continue to pass.

The TDD approach adds compile-time type assertions (using the Equals/AssertTrue pattern already established in claudeBridge.test.ts:421-424) to verify that each Omit<Interface, 'type'> shape is structurally correct. These assertions serve as regression guards: if an interface changes in types.ts, the assertions catch any mismatch at compile time.

steps.description: Add compile-time type assertions to claudeBridge.test.ts verifying each Omit<Interface, 'type'> shape matches the expected payload structure. Import TextDelta, ThinkingDelta, ToolCall, ToolResult, Done, ErrorMessage from sidecar/src/types.ts. Add six AssertTrue<Equals<Omit<X, 'type'>, ExpectedShape>> assertions (e.g., Omit<TextDelta, 'type'> equals { id: string; content: string }). These pass immediately since the interfaces already match the inline shapes — they guard against future drift.

In bridge.ts line 20, expand the import to also bring in TextDelta, ThinkingDelta, ToolCall, ToolResult, Done, ErrorMessage from '../sidecar/src/types'. Then in the entries array (lines 162-187): (a) replace each `as { ... }` inline cast with `as Omit<Interface, 'type'>` — TextDelta for text-delta, ThinkingDelta for thinking-delta, ToolCall for tool-call, ToolResult for tool-result, Done for done, ErrorMessage for error; (b) rename `p` to `payload` in all six destructuring sites for readability per codebase convention.

Run the full test suite (vitest) to verify all existing behavioral tests in claudeBridge.test.ts and bridge.test.ts pass with zero regressions. Also run tsc type-check to confirm the compile-time assertions and new Omit casts are valid.

design_decisions.decision: Use `Omit<Interface, 'type'>` rather than `Pick` or custom helper types
design_decisions.rationale: Omit<X, 'type'> is the most concise and maintainable form — it automatically includes any fields added to the interface in the future, which is exactly the sync-safety benefit we want. Pick would require listing fields explicitly, defeating the purpose.

design_decisions.decision: Rename `p` to `payload` in all six event handler lambdas
design_decisions.rationale: Task description specifies this rename for readability per codebase convention. The variable name `payload` is more descriptive and consistent with the `event.payload` access pattern used elsewhere in bridge.ts.

design_decisions.decision: Add compile-time type assertions rather than runtime tests for the type-level change
design_decisions.rationale: The refactoring is purely at the type level — no runtime behavior changes. Compile-time assertions using the Equals/AssertTrue pattern (already established in claudeBridge.test.ts:421-424) are the appropriate TDD mechanism. They catch structural mismatches at tsc time, not runtime.

design_decisions.decision: Place type assertions in existing claudeBridge.test.ts rather than a new file
design_decisions.rationale: The file already contains compile-time type assertions (the ClaudeMessageContext/MessageContext check at lines 415-424). Colocating related type assertions keeps them discoverable and follows the established pattern.

reuse.how: Reuse the existing `type Equals<A, B>` and `type AssertTrue<T extends true>` definitions already in the file to add new structural assertions for the Omit<Interface, 'type'> shapes.
Import TextDelta, ThinkingDelta, ToolCall, ToolResult, Done, ErrorMessage — these are the canonical source-of-truth interfaces that the inline casts were duplicating.
All 7 event type mapping tests, rollback tests, and edge case tests already exist and serve as regression guards — no new behavioral tests needed.
